paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 30: The Second-Hand Textbook (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary Four girl who buys a second-hand chemistry textbook and discovers the margin notes left by its previous owner, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [19 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2] as in the real paper: 10 short-answer questions (Q1-Q10) — 2 literal, 3 vocabulary-in-context, 3 inference and 2 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q11). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The new chemistry textbook cost twenty-eight dollars, and my mother had already paid for two others that week. So when I saw the second-hand bookshop tucked between a key-cutter and a coffee shop in the void deck near Beauty World, I went in alone, without telling her. The shelves smelled of damp paper and old glue. The uncle behind the counter found me a used copy of the exact edition I needed, its spine creased and its cover softened at the corners, and asked for eight dollars. I paid, and felt, for the first time that month, that I had solved a problem instead of being one.

Paragraph 2
It was only on the bus home that I opened it properly. Someone had written in it — not scribbles, but neat pencil notes crowded into every margin. Beside the section on the mole, a previous owner had drawn a small diagram and written, 'Think of it like a dozen, just bigger. Don't panic.' I almost laughed. Whoever they were, they had panicked once, and then told themselves not to.

Paragraph 3
Over the next weeks the notes became a quiet companion. The previous owner had underlined the words that examiners loved and starred the formulae that always came out. Where the textbook was cold and certain, the margins were warm and uncertain, full of small encouragements written by someone to themselves. 'Slow down here.' 'You always get this one wrong — read it twice.' 'Almost there.' I began to study not just from the book, but alongside the stranger who had owned it before me.

Paragraph 4
There was a name on the inside cover, half-rubbed out, and a year: 2009. I held the page up to the bus window and tried to make out the letters, but only the loop of a single capital survived. I worked out that the owner would be about thirty now, the same age as my cousin who drove a delivery van and complained that he had forgotten everything he had ever learnt in school. I found myself wondering what this stranger had become. A chemist, perhaps, in a white coat in some laboratory I would never see. Or perhaps someone who had failed and walked away from science forever, which somehow seemed a sadder and more interesting possibility.

Paragraph 5
The notes ran out three chapters before the end. After the chapter on rates of reaction, the margins were suddenly blank, the pages crisp and untouched, as though the previous owner had simply stopped one evening and never come back. I sat with that blankness for a long time. It frightened me a little. Had they given up? Had something happened? The book offered no answer; it only went quiet.

Paragraph 6
In the week before my own examination, I did something I had not planned. In the blank margins of those last three chapters, in pencil, I began to write my own notes — copying the previous owner's habit, underlining the examiners' favourite words, starring the formulae. 'Slow down here,' I wrote, in a hand that was trying to be braver than I felt. I was finishing the conversation they had left unfinished.

Paragraph 7
On the morning of the paper, I did not bring the textbook; we were not allowed. But I had read the margins so many times that they came with me anyway, the small pencilled phrases surfacing in my mind as I turned over the question booklet. When a question on rates of reaction appeared — the very chapter where their notes had stopped and mine had begun — I felt, absurdly, that I was not answering it alone. Somewhere behind my own handwriting was a fainter, older hand telling me to slow down and read it twice. I wrote my answer carefully, and I did not panic.

Paragraph 8
I passed. Not brilliantly, but well enough, and better than I had feared. That evening I thought about leaving the textbook back at the second-hand shop, so that the notes — theirs and now mine — might find a third owner, some frightened student a few years from now, opening it on a bus and finding two strangers in the margins telling them to slow down, to read it twice, that they were almost there. I have not decided yet. But I think that is what I will do.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Void deck: the open ground-floor space of a Singapore HDB block, often used for shops and community activities; the mole: a basic unit of amount in chemistry; SERS and other estate terms are not used here.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, how much did the narrator pay for the second-hand textbook? [1]
Q2. From Paragraph 4, what was the year written on the inside cover of the textbook? [1]
Q3. What does the word 'softened' in 'its cover softened at the corners' (Paragraph 1) suggest about the textbook? [1]
questions.answer: it was worn / well-used and no longer new

questions.stem: Q4. What does the phrase 'a quiet companion' (Paragraph 3) suggest about the previous owner's notes? [1]
questions.answer: they gave her comforting, unspoken company

questions.stem: Q5. What does the word 'survived' in 'only the loop of a single capital survived' (Paragraph 4) suggest about the name? [1]
questions.answer: almost all of it had worn / faded away

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain why the narrator went into the bookshop 'alone, without telling her' (Paragraph 1). [2]
questions.answer: ① her mother had already paid for two other textbooks that week; ② she wanted to find a cheaper copy herself so as not to add to the family's spending

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the blankness of the final chapters 'frightened' the narrator (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① it made her imagine the previous owner had given up or that something bad had happened to them; ② facing the same examination herself, the unexplained silence felt unsettling

questions.stem: Q8. Using your own words, explain what the narrator means by 'I was finishing the conversation they had left unfinished' (Paragraph 6). [2]
questions.answer: ① she wrote her own notes in the empty margins where the stranger's notes had stopped; ② so she was continuing and completing what they had begun, like a reply across time

questions.stem: Q9. What is the effect of the contrast in Paragraph 3 between the textbook being 'cold and certain' and the margins being 'warm and uncertain'? [2]
questions.answer: the contrast sets the impersonal printed facts against the human, emotional handwriting — it stresses that her comfort comes not from the book but from the vulnerable, encouraging voice of the stranger

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of describing the blank pages as 'crisp and untouched, as though the previous owner had simply stopped one evening and never come back' (Paragraph 5)? [2]
questions.answer: it makes the disappearance feel sudden and unexplained, almost ominous — 'never came back' hints at an abandoned effort or misfortune, which is what unsettles the narrator

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as she discovers, follows and finally responds to the previous owner's notes. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: resourceful / comforted / uneasy / resolved / embarrassed / bored / impatient / indifferent. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Second-Hand Textbook' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the story unfolds — from feeling resourceful in Paragraph 1, having quietly solved the problem of the costly textbook by buying a cheap second-hand copy herself, to feeling comforted in Paragraphs 2-3 as the previous owner's warm margin notes become a quiet companion, to feeling uneasy in Paragraph 5 when the notes suddenly run out and the blank pages frighten her, ending resolved in Paragraphs 6-8 as she writes her own notes to finish the stranger's unfinished conversation and decides to pass the book on. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 1, having quietly bought a cheap second-hand copy of the textbook herself and feeling she had 'solved a problem instead of being one'? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 2-3, as the previous owner's warm, encouraging margin notes become 'a quiet companion' and she studies alongside the stranger? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5, when the notes suddenly run out and the blank, untouched final chapters frighten her? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 6-8, as she writes her own notes to finish the stranger's unfinished conversation and decides to pass the book on to a future owner? [1]
